items.q: In an exothermic reaction, what happens energetically with the bonds?
items.correct: Energy released forming new bonds exceeds energy absorbed breaking existing bonds.
items.misconception: Bond breaking releases energy and bond making absorbs energy.
items.random_wrong: The reactants get colder because they lose mass as energy.
items.why: Students confuse the direction: they think breaking is 'releasing' (like cracking something open releases contents), when actually breaking always requires energy input.

items.q: Why does benzene undergo substitution rather than addition reactions?
items.correct: Adding across a C=C would destroy the stable delocalised pi system; substitution preserves the aromatic ring.
items.misconception: Benzene contains three normal alternating double bonds and behaves like an alkene with three C=C.
items.random_wrong: Benzene cannot react at all because it's too stable.
items.why: The Kekule diagram shows fixed double bonds, so students assume benzene should behave like cyclohexatriene — missing the resonance/delocalisation that changes its reactivity.

items.q: For a process to be spontaneous, what must happen to the total entropy of the universe?
items.correct: It must increase (ΔS_total > 0) — the system's entropy alone can decrease as long as the surroundings' entropy increases more.
items.misconception: The entropy of the system must increase for the process to be spontaneous.
items.random_wrong: The entropy of the surroundings must stay constant for the process to be spontaneous.
items.why: Students apply the second law only to the system, missing that what counts is system + surroundings (the whole universe) — which is why exothermic reactions can be spontaneous even with ΔS_system < 0.
The answer examiners want is: surroundings warm up. “surroundings cool” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Combustion of methane. “Thermal decomposition of limestone” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If bonds broken cost 1000 kJ/mol and bonds made release 1300 kJ/mol, what is ΔH (kJ/mol)?
items.why: The real figure is -300 kJ/mol. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
